response:
To find the optimal number of practice sessions we analyze the quadratic equation for batting score. The function is \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). This parabola opens downward because the coefficient of \( n^2 \) is negative. The maximum value occurs at the vertex where \( n \) equals negative b over two a. Here b is five and a is negative point two. Calculating this gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \). The result is \( n = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions must be whole numbers we check values near the vertex. At twelve sessions the score is \( 50 + 60 - 28.8 \) which equals eighty point two. At thirteen sessions the score is \( 50 + 65 - 33.8 \) which equals eighty point two as well. Both yield the same maximum average batting score of eighty point two runs. We select twelve as the standard integer solution for practical planning purposes.

Now we calculate the win probability changes using the logistic function provided. The formula is \( P = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}} \). First we determine the score when \( n \) is five. Substituting five gives \( 50 + 25 - 5 \) which equals seventy runs. Next we find the score when \( n \) is ten. Substituting ten gives \( 50 + 50 - 20 \) which equals eighty runs. We now compute the probability for seventy runs. The exponent becomes \( -(0.1 \times 70 - 3) \) which simplifies to negative four. The probability is \( 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \). This value is approximately point nine eight two. Next we compute the probability for eighty runs. The exponent becomes \( -(0.1 \times 80 - 3) \) which simplifies to negative five. The probability is \( 1 / (1 + e^{-5}) \). This value is approximately point nine nine three. The change in win probability is the difference between these two values. Subtracting point nine eight two from point nine nine three yields an increase of roughly point zero one one. This small increase shows diminishing returns as practice sessions grow beyond the optimal point. The coach should focus on quality over quantity to
